skill.name: verification-before-completion
skill.description: Requires fresh verification evidence before an assistant claims code is complete, fixed, passing, or ready for commit.
skill.summary: Verify work with fresh command output before making completion claims.
skill.author: Cygnusfear

security_audit.summary: Static Ruby/shell backtick findings are false positives from markdown fences and examples, not executable code. The weak crypto and reconnaissance findings at SKILL.md lines 3, 71, 111, and 130 have no supporting evidence. The real risk is that the skill intentionally instructs assistants to run fresh verification commands, which is legitimate but requires user awareness.

medium_findings.title: Instruction to Execute Verification Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill directs the assistant to identify and run a full verification command before making completion claims. This is central to the skill and can improve accuracy, but it may execute project test, build, or lint commands selected from the local context.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction to run verification commands is explicit and repeated. Legitimate use is clear, but command execution still carries normal local execution risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Context Command Execution Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill does not hardcode a command, but it tells the assistant to choose and run commands that prove a claim. Users should review commands before execution in untrusted repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is stated directly in the skill text. The exact command depends on the project, so exploitability depends on the surrounding workspace.

content.value_statement: Assistants can overstate completion when tests or builds were not checked. This skill requires fresh verification evidence before any success claim.
actual_capabilities: Requires fresh command output before completion claims.
Prompts the assistant to identify the proof command first.
Requires reading output, exit codes, and failure counts.
Guides truthful reporting when verification fails.
Covers tests, builds, regression checks, requirements, and agent delegation.
limitations: Does not provide project-specific test or build commands.
Does not run commands without the host assistant tools.
Cannot prove correctness beyond the selected verification evidence.
May add time to small or exploratory changes.

use_cases.title: Pre-Commit Verification
use_cases.description: Confirm tests, linting, or builds before saying a code change is ready to commit.

use_cases.title: Agent Result Review
use_cases.description: Check delegated agent work by reviewing diffs and rerunning verification before accepting the result.

use_cases.title: Regression Confidence
use_cases.description: Use red-green checks to prove a regression test fails without the fix and passes with it.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Completion Check
prompt_templates.scenario: Use before reporting a small task as complete.
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply verification-before-completion. Identify the command that proves this task is complete, run it, read the output, and report only the verified status.

prompt_templates.title: Bug Fix Verification
prompt_templates.scenario: Use after changing code for a reported bug.
prompt_templates.prompt: Before claiming the bug is fixed, verify the original symptom. Run the most relevant test or reproduction and state the evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Commit Readiness Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Use before committing or creating a pull request.
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether this change is ready to commit. Run the required verification commands, inspect the diff, and report remaining risks.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Regression Proof
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when a regression test was added.
prompt_templates.prompt: Verify the regression test with a red-green cycle. Show that it fails without the fix, passes with the fix, and supports the completion claim.

output_examples.input: Can I say the tests pass now?
output_examples.output: Not yet. I need fresh test output from this turn before making that claim.

output_examples.input: The agent says the refactor is complete.
output_examples.output: I will inspect the diff and run the relevant verification before accepting that status.

output_examples.input: Report whether the bug is fixed.
output_examples.output: The fix is verified only after the original failure case passes with current command output.

best_practices: Choose the narrowest command that directly proves the claim.
Read the full output and exit status before summarizing results.
Report failures and gaps plainly instead of softening the status.
anti_patterns: Claiming success from memory or an earlier run.
Treating lint success as proof that tests or builds pass.
Accepting delegated agent reports without checking the actual changes.

faq.question: Does this skill run tests automatically?
faq.answer: No. It instructs the assistant to run relevant verification commands when tools and permissions allow it.

faq.question: Is this only for software tests?
faq.answer: No. It also applies to builds, linting, requirements checks, regression proof, and delegated agent review.

faq.question: Can it choose the right command for every repository?
faq.answer: No. The assistant must infer commands from project files, scripts, documentation, or user guidance.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill intentionally encourages local command execution. This is useful, but commands should be reviewed in untrusted projects.

faq.question: Does it guarantee the work is correct?
faq.answer: No. It improves honesty by tying claims to evidence, but verification quality depends on the selected checks.

faq.question: When should I use it?
faq.answer: Use it before any statement that work is complete, fixed, passing, ready to commit, or ready for review.
